title: Cap pages per authority per seed-poll cycle to bound budget
description:
Seed cycles on backlogged authorities currently thrash: PlanIt 429s mid-pagination, partial HWM saved, next cycle re-fetches the same same-day pages (because `different_start` is date-truncated per PlanIt docs — verified 2026-04-20), burns the rate budget before advancing. Net effect: only ~1 authority polled per seed cycle (vs designed ~8), 100% rate_limited termination, full rotation would take ~9 days not 26h.

## Design

Cap the per-authority pagination in `PlanItClient.FetchApplicationsAsync` at N pages per call (proposal: **N=3 → 300 apps max per authority per cycle**).

- Add `int? maxPages` param (nullable so existing `SearchApplicationsAsync` callers and tests aren't forced to specify).
- Exit the `do...while` pagination loop when `page > maxPages`, regardless of whether PlanIt signalled end-of-data.
- Handler sets `completedSuccessfully = true` on natural loop exit (existing behaviour). Page-capped exit looks the same to the handler.
- HWM already advances via `highWaterMark = max(LastDifferent)` from streamed apps (PollPlanItCommandHandler.cs:90-93, saved at line 148).

## Why this works

- **Deterministic breadth**: each seed cycle processes min(remaining_budget / per_authority_time, N_authorities). Backlog authorities can't eat the whole cycle.
- **Forward progress guaranteed**: bailing at page 3 (intentionally) saves HWM from app #300. Next rotation asks PlanIt for `different_start = date_of_app_300`, skips fully-processed days. Re-fetch overlap limited to same-day apps (dedupe via `HasSameBusinessFieldsAs` handles writes; read cost bounded).
- **Backlog drains over rotations**: 1,200-app authority drains in 4 rotations (~4-5 days at 26h/rotation). Matches seed-polling spec: 'catalogue deepens naturally over subsequent daily rotations'.
- **No PlanIt policy change needed**: we're reducing our demand, not negotiating.

## Sites to touch

- `api/src/town-crier.infrastructure/PlanIt/PlanItClient.cs` — add `maxPages` to `IPlanItClient.FetchApplicationsAsync` signature + implementation.
- `api/src/town-crier.application/PlanIt/IPlanItClient.cs` — update interface.
- `api/src/town-crier.application/Polling/PollPlanItCommandHandler.cs:86` — pass the configured cap.
- Config: new `Polling:MaxPagesPerAuthorityPerCycle` setting, wired via `town-crier.worker/Program.cs`. Default 3.
- Tests: TUnit cases for (a) cap hit mid-pagination (returns partial, no error), (b) cap not hit (natural exit unchanged), (c) null cap = unbounded (regression guard for Search path).

## Acceptance

- New config value defaults to 3 in prod appsettings.
- Unit test: 5-page fake PlanIt response + cap=3 yields exactly 300 records, HWM = LastDifferent of 300th app.
- Post-deploy telemetry (24h):
  - Seed cycles: termination shifts from 100% `rate_limited` → majority `natural`
  - `authorities_polled` per seed cycle ≥ 5 (up from 1)
  - PlanIt 429 rate drops

## Follow-up (out of scope)

- Fail-fast on 429 inside `PlanItClient.SendWithThrottleAsync` (plan B) — less urgent once C is in place, but still a resilience win.
- Consider per-authority time budget in addition to page cap.

title: Shrink seed-cycle first-touch backfill window from 24h to 1h
description:
Seed cycles are hitting 600s replica timeout after polling only ONE authority per 30-min window (vs designed ~8/cycle).

Telemetry (2026-04-20, ~4h post-deploy of #259-#261):
- Watched cycles: 100% natural, 8 authorities/cycle, ~33s per authority
- Seed cycles: 100% rate_limited, 1 authority/cycle, 242s per authority, FAILED with 429
- Apps ingested per seed authority: ~1,200 (first-touch backfill burst)
- 8× HTTP 429 from PlanIt in 12h, all in seed windows

Root cause: api/src/town-crier.application/Polling/PollPlanItCommandHandler.cs:84 — `lastPollTime ??= now.AddDays(-1)` gives first-touch authorities a 24h backfill window. PlanIt returns ~1,200 apps, Polly retries chew through the cycle budget on 429s, only 1 authority completes before timeout.

At 1 authority per 30-min seed window, full 417-authority rotation takes ~9 days instead of the designed ~26 hours.

Fix: change line 84 to `lastPollTime ??= now.AddHours(-1)`. Matches the 15-min cadence more realistically; catalogue deepens over subsequent rotations (acceptable per seed-polling spec: 'paying users expected no sooner than ~1 month out').

Spec: docs/specs/seed-polling.md (update the 'First-sync window' section from 24h → 1h).

Acceptance:
- Line 84 uses AddHours(-1)
- Spec updated
- No existing tests assert the 24h default (grep confirmed), so no test breakage expected
- Verify post-deploy: seed cycles terminate 'natural' and polled-authorities-per-cycle > 1
close_reason: Verified via PlanIt API docs (https://www.planit.org.uk/api): 'all dates in YYYY-MM-DD or DD/MM/YYYY format'. The differentStart param in PlanItClient.cs:108 is already formatted yyyy-MM-dd — changing the caller from AddDays(-1) to AddHours(-1) has no effect on the wire. Need a different strategy (cap apps/authority per cycle, short-circuit 429s, or page-limit per authority).

title: Remove Application Insights JS from web frontend
description: The web frontend initialises @microsoft/applicationinsights-web in src/telemetry.ts with enableAutoRouteTracking=true. Every page load sets telemetry identifiers (ai_user, ai_session) and sends per-user telemetry to Azure Monitor. Under PECR reg 6 this counts as non-essential first-party analytics and requires consent. Backend (server-side) App Insights is unaffected and remains in place on a legitimate-interests basis. Decision: remove web-side App Insights entirely. For a pre-live app the client-side telemetry provides near-zero value, and when real users arrive a minimal consent-free error reporter is a cleaner rebuild than trying to gate the current setup behind a banner. Found by /legal audit on 2026-04-19.
design: Remove web-side Application Insights entirely. (1) Delete web/src/telemetry.ts and any imports of it. (2) Remove @microsoft/applicationinsights-web and @microsoft/applicationinsights-react-js (if present) from web/package.json dependencies, then re-lock. (3) Remove the telemetry provider from web/src/App.tsx and any usages (e.g., useAppInsightsContext, withAITracking HOCs, TrackEvent calls). (4) Remove the VITE_APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING env var from .env files, Static Web App app settings, and Pulumi config — confirm nothing else reads it. (5) Verify build passes with no remaining references. Server-side App Insights (api + worker) is unaffected — do not touch. Do NOT replace with a minimal-mode config; the decision is removal, not reduction. When real users arrive a consent-free error reporter (e.g., Sentry with no cookies/IP/userId, or a custom endpoint) can be scoped as a separate bead.

title: Self-host Google Fonts to remove third-party US transfer from web
description: The web frontend loads the Inter font from fonts.googleapis.com and fonts.gstatic.com (index.html lines 21-25). Every page load transfers the user's IP address to Google (US) without consent. Under PECR and UK GDPR Chapter V this is a non-essential third-party transfer. Self-hosting eliminates the transfer entirely and avoids needing a cookie banner for it. Found by /legal audit on 2026-04-19.
design: Download the Inter woff2 subsets we actually use, place under web/public/fonts/, and replace the <link> tags in web/index.html with a local @font-face declaration in the global stylesheet. Remove the preconnect hints to Google domains. Verify font fallback still works in dev and build. This removes the only third-party resource on the web and removes Google as a processor from the privacy-policy disclosure.

title: iOS delete account must call DELETE /v1/me before clearing Auth0 session
description: iOS SettingsViewModel.confirmDeleteAccount currently only clears the local Auth0 session and keychain. It does NOT call the server-side DELETE /v1/me endpoint. Users who tap 'Delete Account' leave their server-side data intact, which breaks UK GDPR Art. 17 erasure. Found by /legal audit on 2026-04-19.
design: File: mobile/ios/packages/town-crier-presentation/Sources/Features/Settings/SettingsViewModel.swift around lines 113-132. Sequence must be: (1) call APIUserProfileRepository.delete() — this already exists and hits DELETE /v1/me; (2) only on 204 success, call Auth0AuthenticationService.deleteAccount() to clear keychain + session; (3) on failure, surface the error and do NOT clear local credentials (user stays logged in and can retry). Depends on the server-side cascade — only fully meaningful once the account-deletion-cascade bead lands. Add a unit test that verifies repository.delete is called before auth.deleteAccount and that local credentials are NOT cleared on server error.

title: Cascade user deletion across all Cosmos containers and Auth0
description: DeleteUserProfileCommandHandler currently deletes only the Users document. It does not cascade to WatchZones, DeviceRegistrations, SavedApplications, DecisionAlerts, or OfferCodes.RedeemedByUserId, and does not delete the Auth0 identity. Under UK GDPR Art. 17 (right to erasure), deleting an account must remove all associated personal data. Found by /legal audit on 2026-04-19.
design: On DELETE /v1/me, the handler must: (1) delete all WatchZones for userId; (2) delete all DeviceRegistrations for userId; (3) delete all SavedApplications for userId; (4) delete all DecisionAlerts for userId; (5) let Notifications TTL out naturally; (6) for OfferCodes where RedeemedByUserId==userId, anonymise the field (set to null or a sentinel like 'deleted-user') rather than deleting the ledger entry — preserve billing audit trail; (7) call Auth0ManagementClient to delete the Auth0 user. Each step idempotent. userId is the partition key on user-scoped containers, so these are point-deletes or per-partition queries. Rollback is not possible once started — document as intentional. Integration test: create data in every user-scoped container, call DELETE /v1/me, assert all containers empty for that user and Auth0 user is deleted.
notes: Autopilot: failed 2026-04-19. Reason: worker ran out of budget mid-implementation. Committed OfferCode.AnonymiseRedemption domain method (commits 58aefc0, 7638d73) but cascade delete (WatchZone/Device/SavedApp/DecisionAlert repositories, Auth0ManagementClient.DeleteUserAsync, DeleteUserProfileCommandHandler wiring) is uncommitted. Needs human triage — consider splitting into smaller beads: (a) add idempotent Delete methods to each user-scoped repository, (b) add Auth0ManagementClient.DeleteUserAsync, (c) wire cascade + OfferCode.AnonymiseRedemption into DeleteUserProfileCommandHandler.
close_reason: Superseded by tc-j2fk (Auth0 delete) and tc-sy7c (cascade delete) from /legal audit 2026-04-20.

title: Move Azure Communication Services to UK data location
description: Azure Communication Services (CommunicationService + EmailService + Domain) is currently provisioned with DataLocation="Europe" in SharedStack.cs (lines 226-252). Email content and recipient addresses are stored in EEA data centres, not the UK. This is inconsistent with our stated data residency (UK South for everything else) and creates an unnecessary cross-border transfer disclosure for UK GDPR. Found by /legal audit on 2026-04-19.
design: Azure does NOT allow mutating DataLocation on an existing ACS resource — it is immutable after creation. Migration path: (1) Provision new CommunicationService, EmailService, and Domain resources in SharedStack.cs with DataLocation="UK" under a different resource name (e.g., acs-town-crier-uk). Keep Location="global" (ACS control plane is always global). (2) Re-verify the towncrierapp.uk domain on the new EmailService — Azure issues new DKIM/SPF/TXT records that must be added to Cloudflare DNS since DomainManagement=CustomerManaged. (3) Update the ACS connection string secret in Key Vault and any app config referencing it (AcsEmailSender in api/src/town-crier.infrastructure/Notifications/). (4) Redeploy API + worker via CI/CD so they pick up the new connection string. (5) Destroy the old acs-town-crier-shared, email-town-crier-shared, and the Europe-hosted Domain resources. Pre-live users means no migration window concerns.
notes: Files to change: infra/SharedStack.cs (ACS + EmailService + Domain blocks). Connection string secret in Key Vault. Pulumi.shared.yaml may need a config flag if we want a gradual switchover, but for pre-live state a single atomic change is fine. After landing, re-run /legal to remove EEA disclosure from the privacy policy.

title: Dedupe planning applications before Cosmos upsert in poll cycle
description:
Poll cycle currently upserts every record PlanIt returns, including bulk reindex floods where thousands of records are semantically unchanged. Observed prod: 1 authority (auth 494 = British Islands cross-border grouping) returned 7326 records for a 1-day different_start window — most were 2021-2024 applications whose last_different field was bumped en masse by a PlanIt rescrape. Every one triggered a Cosmos upsert + per-record FindZonesContainingAsync lookup (~30-50ms each), blowing the 120s poll replica timeout.

Fix: before upserting, compare incoming PlanningApplication against the stored Cosmos document. Skip UpsertAsync AND the zone-matching/notification fan-out when nothing material changed. Comparison should be over the business fields (address, description, app_state, app_type, decided_date, etc.) — NOT last_different/last_changed/last_scraped which are PlanIt bookkeeping.

Affects PollPlanItCommandHandler.cs per-record loop. Applies to BOTH watched and seed cycles — reindex pollution affects any authority once it has an active watch zone. This is the load-bearing fix; bd tc-ethl replica-timeout follow-up is only a safety net.

Acceptance: reindex floods are ingested in time proportional to materially-changed records, not total records returned. Unit test with a handler scenario where repository returns the same app twice — second call must skip upsert + zone lookup.

title: fix(worker): ship appsettings.json so HttpClient/Polly log suppression actually applies
description:
The worker project (api/src/town-crier.worker/town-crier.worker.csproj) uses Microsoft.NET.Sdk, which does NOT auto-copy appsettings.json to publish output. Dockerfile.worker only publishes the AOT binary, so the config never lands in the container. Host.CreateApplicationBuilder falls back to Default: Information for all categories — the System.Net.Http.HttpClient / Polly suppression from PR #237 is a no-op in prod.

Evidence from Log Analytics (last 24h, AppRoleName=town-crier-worker):
- System.Net.Http.HttpClient.CosmosRest.LogicalHandler: 164,288 Info traces
- System.Net.Http.HttpClient.CosmosRest.ClientHandler: 164,287 Info traces
- Polly: 82,141 Info traces
The same filter works in town-crier-web (Sdk.Web auto-copies appsettings.json) — zero HttpClient traces there.

Impact: ~410K redundant Info traces/day in AppTraces plus duplicate lines in ContainerAppConsoleLogs_CL. Post-PR-#237 ingestion settled at ~0.77 GB/day on Apr 17, still close to the 1 GB cap. Projected drop after fix: ~0.4–0.5 GB/day (to ~0.3 GB/day total).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Add <Content Include="appsettings*.json" CopyToOutputDirectory="PreserveNewest" /> to town-crier.worker.csproj
- [ ] Verify appsettings.json is present at /app in the published image (docker build + inspect, or local publish)
- [ ] Also add Polly: Warning to town-crier.web/appsettings.json (minor — 429/day today)
- [ ] After prod deploy, confirm in AppTraces that System.Net.Http.HttpClient.CosmosRest.* and Polly Info traces drop to ~0 for AppRoleName=town-crier-worker
- [ ] Confirm daily Usage (DataType=AppTraces + ContainerAppConsoleLogs_CL) drops meaningfully vs. the Apr 17 baseline
close_reason: Shipped via PR #251 (commit 56d4481). Verified post-deploy: zero System.Net.Http.HttpClient.CosmosRest.* or Polly Info traces for AppRoleName=town-crier-worker in the 15 min after deploy (was ~410K/day). Only Microsoft.Hosting.Lifetime and TownCrier.Worker remain — the intended survivors.

title: iOS: zone picker chips invisible but tappable on Applications page (regression)
description:
Zone picker renders as an empty gap between the navigation title and the status filter pills on the Applications page. Chips are tappable (zone selection works) but visually invisible across all appearance modes (light, dark, system, OLED). The status filter chips directly below — which use identical styling code (same colors, same .buttonStyle(.plain), same capsule shape, same modifier chain) — render correctly.

Previously filed as tc-29z1, which was closed as fixed by tc-nvgw (Color.themed() refactored to UIColor(dynamicProvider:)). That fix did not resolve the underlying rendering issue. The autopilot worker also flagged this as needing hands-on simulator debugging.

Root cause analysis: The only structural difference between the working filter chips and the broken zone picker is that ZonePickerView is a separate View struct, while filterHeader builds its chips inline in ApplicationListView. Both use ForEach→Button→Text with .font/.foregroundStyle/.padding/.background/.clipShape/.overlay/.buttonStyle(.plain). This points to a SwiftUI rendering bug where .foregroundStyle() and .background() inside Button labels are not applied when the Button is in a ForEach inside a ScrollView inside a separate View struct — possibly related to how SwiftUI optimizes rendering across View boundaries.

Files: ZonePickerView.swift (the invisible component), ApplicationListView.swift:82-92 (where it's embedded), MapView.swift:90-100 (same component used on Map tab — check if also affected).

Potential fixes to try in the simulator: (1) inline the zone picker code directly into ApplicationListView, bypassing the separate View struct; (2) replace Button+label with a view+onTapGesture to avoid buttonStyle interaction; (3) add explicit .tint() or .renderingMode() to force text rendering.
close_reason: Shipped in PR #241. Root cause: .navigationBarTitleDisplayMode(.large) hijacked zone picker ScrollView. Fix: single List as sole scroll container with inlined chips.

title: fix(ios): watch zone name edit not persisting in list view after save
description:
When editing a watch zone name in the iOS app, the PATCH API call succeeds (name is saved server-side), but the WatchZoneListView never refreshes to show the updated name. The user sees the old name until they force-reload (e.g., restart the app or switch tabs).

Root cause: AppCoordinator.makeWatchZoneEditorViewModel() line 252-258 — the onSave callback calls watchZoneRepository.loadAll() but discards the result (_ = try? await). The WatchZoneListViewModel is a separate object with its own zones array, and its load() is only called via .task on first appear. After the edit sheet dismisses, nobody tells the list view model to reload.

Fix: The coordinator needs to signal the WatchZoneListViewModel to reload after a successful zone edit. Options include adding a refresh callback, using .task(id:) with a trigger, or leveraging the sheet's onDismiss parameter to trigger viewModel.load().

title: iOS: zone picker chips invisible on Applications page (dark mode color bug)
description: On the Applications page, the zone selector is invisible — empty space where it should be, but clicking in the space selects a zone. User is in LIGHT MODE (not dark mode). Initial dark-mode color theory was ruled out — Color.themed() always returns light hex values, and the light palette should be visible (dark text 0x1C1917 on white 0xFFFFFF with tan border 0xE8E4DF). The filter header chips use identical styling (same colors, same button style, same capsule shape) and render correctly. ZonePickerView is a separate View struct; filterHeader is inline — otherwise structurally identical. Root cause needs hands-on simulator debugging. Files: ZonePickerView.swift, ApplicationListView.swift, ApplicationListViewModel.swift
notes: Autopilot: blocked 2026-04-14. Reason: root cause unidentified — needs hands-on simulator debugging. Worker cannot diagnose visual rendering issues without simulator.
close_reason: Fixed by tc-nvgw — Color.themed() now uses UIColor(dynamicProvider:) instead of raw SwiftUI Color(hex:), resolving the rendering issue in ScrollView/Button context

title: Bug: 'Something went wrong' error on both Applications and Map after saving
description: After saving an application from the list detail view, navigating to the Map tab shows a 'Something went wrong' error. After force-quitting and relaunching, BOTH the Applications and Map tabs show this error persistently. Error display uses ErrorStateView which renders DomainError.userTitle (ErrorStateView.swift:21). Both ViewModels have @Published var error: DomainError? that gets set via handleError() when loadApplications() fails. Since both screens share the same underlying APIClient (URLSessionAPIClient), a persistent API failure (e.g. expired auth token, server error) would cause both to error simultaneously. Interestingly, after the error the status filter chips DO appear on the map (meaning tier resolved correctly and canFilter is true), but zone filters are still missing (meaning zone loading failed — zones array is empty). The save operation itself (PUT /v1/me/saved-applications/{uid}) should not affect subsequent GET calls, unless it triggers an auth state change or the server starts returning errors. Investigation needed: (1) check API logs for errors around the time of the save, (2) check if auth token refresh is failing, (3) reproduce with network logging enabled. Key files: ApplicationListViewModel.swift:159-161, MapViewModel.swift:119-121, APISavedApplicationRepository.swift:20-30, ErrorStateView.swift, DomainError.swift:46-50.
notes:
## Investigation (updated with user context)

**User confirmed: persistent after restart, dev environment (simulator).**

### Shared infrastructure:
Both ApplicationListViewModel and MapViewModel use the same URLSessionAPIClient (via APIPlanningApplicationRepository and APIWatchZoneRepository). If the API client enters a bad state (e.g. expired auth token that can't be refreshed), all API calls fail simultaneously.

### Error propagation:
Both ViewModels have `@Published var error: DomainError?` set via handleError(). ErrorStateView renders DomainError.userTitle which returns 'Something Went Wrong' for .unexpected, .invalidCoordinate, etc. (DomainError.swift:46-50).

### Persistent across restart:
If the error persists after force-quit and relaunch, it's NOT transient (not a race condition). Possible causes:
1. Auth token stored in Keychain is expired/invalid and refresh fails → sessionExpired error on every API call
2. Dev API server is actually down or returning errors consistently
3. User profile or data is in a bad state server-side causing 500s

### Status filters showing after error:
User reports status filters appear on map after the error. This means:
- subscriptionTier resolved to non-.free at some point (at least once)
- The .id() modifier triggered view recreation with correct tier
- But subsequent API calls still fail → error state shows with filter chips above it

### Zone filters missing after error:
If loadApplications() fails at the zone loading step (watchZoneRepository.loadAll() throws), zones array stays empty → showZonePicker = false. The error is caught and shown in the list/map body area, but the zone picker area above stays empty.

### Trigger: save operation
The save operation (PUT /v1/me/saved-applications/{uid}) itself is unlikely to corrupt state. BUT: if the save call triggers a token refresh that fails, the API client might enter a bad state where subsequent calls use an expired token.

### Debug steps:
1. Check dev API health: curl https://api-dev.towncrierapp.uk/health
2. Check Xcode console for auth errors or HTTP status codes
3. Try logging out and back in to get a fresh token
4. Check if the error is 'Session Expired' or 'Something Went Wrong' specifically
close_reason: Fixed token refresh error handling: network failures during refresh now throw networkUnavailable (retryable) instead of sessionExpired; retry-after-refresh errors propagate correctly; sessionExpired is now retryable. 3 new tests added.

title: Bug: Status filter chips not rendering — only Saved chip shows
description: On both ApplicationListView and MapView, the filter bar shows only the Saved chip. Status filters (All, Pending, Approved, Refused, Withdrawn, Appealed) are gated behind viewModel.canFilter which returns tier != .free (ApplicationListViewModel.swift:34, MapViewModel.swift:37). If the subscription tier resolves to .free (either because the user IS on free tier, or because resolveSubscriptionTier fails and defaults to .free), status chips won't render. The .id(coordinator.subscriptionTier) modifier (TownCrierApp.swift:131,148) is supposed to force view recreation when tier resolves, but if all three tier sources (JWT, StoreKit, server profile) return .free or error out, the tier stays .free and .id() doesn't trigger recreation. Investigation needed: (1) confirm user's actual subscription tier, (2) check if resolveSubscriptionTier() is succeeding, (3) verify .id() modifier correctly recreates views when tier changes from .free to non-free. Key files: TownCrierApp.swift:108,131,148, AppCoordinator.swift:165-174, ApplicationListView.swift:102, MapView.swift:110.
notes:
## Investigation (updated with user context)

**User confirmed: Pro tier, 7 watch zones, dev environment (simulator).**

### Tier resolution pipeline (AppCoordinator.swift:165-174):
Three sources, all can silently fall back to .free:

1. **JWT claim** (JWTSubscriptionTierExtractor.swift:13-20): Extracts `subscription_tier` from access token. Returns .free if claim is missing. Requires an Auth0 Action to populate this claim — likely NOT configured for dev tenant.

2. **StoreKit** (StoreKitSubscriptionService): `currentEntitlement()` returns nil on simulator (no real purchases) → defaults to .free.

3. **Server profile** (AppCoordinator.swift:176-188): Calls `GET /v1/me` → ServerProfileDTO.toDomain() → SubscriptionTier(rawValue: tier.lowercased()). Catches ALL errors and returns .free. If the profile tier field doesn't match 'pro'/'personal' exactly, rawValue init returns nil → .free.

### Result:
`max(.free, .free, .free) = .free` → canFilter = false → only Saved chip renders.

### Most likely root cause:
On the simulator, the ONLY reliable tier source is the server profile (GET /v1/me). If this endpoint returns tier='pro', it should work. Debug by checking: (1) does GET /v1/me return a profile? (2) what tier string does it return? (3) does rawValue parsing succeed?

### Files:
- JWTSubscriptionTierExtractor.swift:13-20 (JWT claim extraction)
- AppCoordinator.swift:165-188 (triple-source resolution)
- APIUserProfileRepository.swift:23-34,97-105 (server profile fetch + tier parsing)
- TownCrierApp.swift:108,131,148 (.task trigger and .id modifier)
close_reason: Fixed tier resolution: fetchServerTier() now returns nil on error (not .free), preserving previous tier. Added UserDefaults-backed tier cache so paying users retain features across launches. 6 new tests.

title: iOS: Applications tab shows 'No Connection' when internet is available
description:
All API errors (server errors, decoding failures, etc.) are misclassified as network connectivity issues. Every repository's catch block maps any non-DomainError to DomainError.networkUnavailable, including APIError.serverError and APIError.decodingFailed.

## Root Cause (confirmed via App Insights)

The iOS app sends GET /v1/applications?authorityId= (empty string) because TownCrierApp.swift initializes ApplicationListViewModel with LocalAuthority(code: '', name: '') in the release (non-DEBUG) build path. The API endpoint expects an int authorityId, so ASP.NET returns HTTP 400 Bad Request instantly — no handler code is reached.

APIPlanningApplicationRepository catches this 400 as a generic error and remaps it to DomainError.networkUnavailable, which the UI renders as the 'No Connection' WiFi-slash screen.

## App Insights Evidence (2026-04-12)

| Time (UTC) | Endpoint | Status | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| 07:34:44 | GET /v1/applications?authorityId= | 400 | 191ms |
| 07:34:46 | GET /v1/applications?authorityId= | 400 | 0.6ms |
| 07:34:50 | GET /v1/applications?authorityId=<valid> | 200 | 2896ms |
| 07:36:31 | GET /v1/applications?authorityId= | 400 | 0.5ms |

3 out of 4 requests failed with 400 due to empty authorityId. The one request with a valid authority ID succeeded.

## Two-Part Fix Required

1. **Application loading**: Replace the hardcoded empty LocalAuthority in the release build path (TownCrierApp.swift lines 70-71) with proper authority loading from GET /v1/me/application-authorities — same issue as tc-d6m.
2. **Error mapping**: Repositories should distinguish HTTP 400/500 (APIError) from actual network failures (URLError). A 400 Bad Request is not 'network unavailable'. The NWPathConnectivityMonitor exists but is never consulted.

## Files
- TownCrierApp.swift (lines 70-71) — empty LocalAuthority in release path
- APIPlanningApplicationRepository.swift (lines 19-23) — catch-all error mapping
- All 18 repository files with the same catch { throw DomainError.networkUnavailable } pattern
- URLSessionAPIClient.swift (lines 25-42) — only catches URLError, lets APIError bubble up
close_reason: Fixed: two-part fix — (1) Error+DomainMapping.swift correctly maps APIError vs URLError across all 8 repositories, (2) ApplicationListViewModel now loads authorities dynamically instead of using empty LocalAuthority. 24 new tests.

title: [SRE] Poll job killed by replicaTimeout — uncapped Retry-After delay
description:
**What:** Every prod poll job execution since 2026-04-06T23:00Z has failed (33+ consecutive failures). The job processes authorities for ~6 minutes, hits PlanIt 429 for authority 52, then blindly honors the Retry-After header (281-554 seconds). The 10-minute replicaTimeout kills the container mid-wait.

**KQL (console logs):**
```
ContainerAppConsoleLogs_CL
| where ContainerGroupName_s startswith 'job-town-crier-poll-prod'
| where Log_s has 'waiting' and Log_s has '429'
| project TimeGenerated, Log_s
| order by TimeGenerated desc
```

**Key data:**
- Last successful run: 2026-04-06T22:00Z (33h ago)
- replicaTimeout: 600s (10 min)
- Retry-After values from PlanIt: 281-554s (4.7-9.2 min)
- Authority 52 is the consistent trigger
- Both prod and dev environments affected
- Root cause: PR #197 (aeb3ea5) added Retry-After header parsing without a max cap

**Blast radius:** Complete data ingestion outage. Zero planning applications being synced to prod. Dashboard shows flatline for sync success/failure and applications ingested.

**Investigation starting point:** PlanItClient.SendWithRetryAsync() in api/src/town-crier.infrastructure/PlanIt/PlanItClient.cs — the Retry-After delay must be capped to a value well under the 600s replicaTimeout (e.g. 30-60s max). Consider also: (1) after capping, should the code skip the authority rather than retry? (2) should the handler break out of the polling loop on rate-limit like it does after 2 consecutive 429s?
close_reason: Fixed: capped Retry-After delay to 30s max via MaxRetryAfterSeconds on PlanItRetryOptions. 6 commits, 150 tests pass.

title: [SRE] PlanIt 429 exceptions bubbling past retry loop in worker
description:
PlanIt API is returning HTTP 429 on 15.38% of outbound calls (8 of 52) since last prod deploy at 2026-04-19T19:52:16Z. The retry loop (towncrier.planit.retries = 6 across 2 retry events) recovers some, but 4 HttpRequestException records reach App Insights as unhandled in town-crier-worker:

  System.Net.Http.HttpRequestException: 429 (Too Many Requests)
    at TownCrier.Infrastructure.PlanIt.PlanItClient.FetchApplicationsAsync
    at TownCrier.Application.Polling.PollPlanItCommandHandler.HandleAsync

Timestamps: 2026-04-20T09:50:32Z and 2026-04-20T10:19:17Z (clustered, suggesting retry exhaustion within a single polling cycle).

Blast radius: data freshness only — no user-facing request path affected. Polling business metrics:
- towncrier.planit.http_errors = 8
- towncrier.planit.retries = 6 (2 retry events)
- towncrier.polling.rate_limited = 2 authorities skipped
- towncrier.polling.applications_ingested = 3122 (still succeeding overall)

Suggested investigation: review retry/backoff policy in PlanItClient — either the retry budget is too small to cover PlanIt's rate-limit windows, or the 429 error should be swallowed at the PollPlanItCommandHandler level and recorded as a skip rather than rethrown as an exception.

KQL:
  exceptions
  | where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-19T19:52:16Z)
  | where type == 'System.Net.Http.HttpRequestException'
  | summarize count_ = count(), lastSeen = max(timestamp) by type, outerMessage

  customMetrics
  | where name in ('http.client.request.duration')
  | where customDimensions['server.address'] == 'www.planit.org.uk'
  | summarize requests = sum(valueCount) by tostring(customDimensions['http.response.status_code'])

title: Graceful timeout + success semantics for poll cycles
description:
Seed poll cycles (cycle.type="seed", every :15 and :45) run through all 417 authorities in LRU order until Container Apps hits replicaTimeout=600s and SIGTERMs the process. Result: every seed cycle is recorded as Failed even when the run successfully ingested thousands of applications. Example prod run at 13:15 UTC 2026-04-19 polled 60 authorities / 2,822 applications with zero dependency failures, still marked Failed.

Root cause:
- api/src/town-crier.worker/Program.cs:191 passes CancellationToken.None into pollHandler.HandleAsync.
- api/src/town-crier.application/Polling/PollPlanItCommandHandler.cs:58 iterates sortedIds with foreach — never observes cancellation, so the process is killed mid-loop.

Fix:
1. Worker: create a CancellationTokenSource bounded to (replicaTimeout - grace). replicaTimeout is known at build time (600s) but should come from config (e.g. POLL_REPLICA_TIMEOUT_SECONDS) to avoid drift from infra. Grace ~= 30s for OTel flush.
2. Handler: check ct.IsCancellationRequested at the top of the foreach loop (before starting a new authority). On cancellation, break out cleanly and return the partial PollPlanItResult.
3. Worker: redefine exit code semantics. Exit 0 when EITHER:
   - result.ApplicationCount > 0 (we ingested new apps), OR
   - no per-authority error was logged during the run (clean pass even with zero new apps).
   Exit 1 only when we did no useful work AND hit errors. Rate-limited stops remain exit 0.
4. Expose a counter like towncrier.polling.cycles_completed{cycle.type, termination="natural|time_bounded|rate_limited"} so we can tell graceful-timeouts apart from real failures in telemetry.

Acceptance:
- Seed runs that reach the time boundary exit 0 and register as Succeeded in Container Apps.
- Watched runs still exit 0 with the same logic (unchanged behaviour).
- A seed run where PlanIt returns 500 on every authority exits 1.
- Unit test: FakeCycleSelector + CancellationToken cancelled mid-loop returns PollPlanItResult with the partial counts and no exception.

Related: PRs #259 (alternating cycles) and #260 (replicaTimeout 120→600). Current behaviour was discovered 2026-04-19 verifying the first seed run after the 12:52 UTC infra update.

title: Raise poll-job replicaTimeout from 120s to 600s
description:
infra/EnvironmentStack.cs:337 hardcodes replicaTimeout=120 for the poll Container Apps Job. Sized for the original watched-only cycle (~8 authorities in ~60s). Since tc-ethl landed (alternating seed cycles over 417 authority IDs), every seed run (:15 and :45) is killed by Azure's DeadlineExceeded rule — confirmed in ContainerAppSystemLogs ('Job was active longer than specified deadline').

Fix: bump to 600s to match the digest job. This is a safety-net only — the primary fix is dedupe-before-upsert (tc-yt57). Without dedupe, 600s still isn't enough for heavy reindex days; with dedupe, 600s is generous headroom.

Single-line infra change in EnvironmentStack.cs. Ship via PR + Pulumi CD as per CLAUDE.md 'Deployments — PR-Only Policy'. No direct pulumi up.

title: fix(infra): tc-i103 Pulumi up failed — Table resource conflicts with Azure-managed ContainerAppConsoleLogs
description:
PR #254 / tc-i103 partially landed. The shared stack apply (CD Dev, run id 24604207614) failed mid-update with:

  cannot create already existing resource '/subscriptions/.../workspaces/log-town-crier-shared/tables/ContainerAppConsoleLogs'

Root cause: when ManagedEnvironment.appLogsConfiguration.destination flips from 'log-analytics' to 'azure-monitor', Azure automatically provisions the native (non-_CL) ContainerAppConsoleLogs table in the target workspace. The Pulumi 'azure-native:operationalinsights:Table table-containerappconsolelogs-basic' resource then can't create — it already exists with a default plan.

Pulumi aborted on this first error, so the DiagnosticSetting 'diag-cae-town-crier-shared' was also NEVER created (verified via az rest GET …/diagnosticSettings → empty array).

Current state in Azure:
- Env destination: azure-monitor ✓ (applied successfully)
- _CL legacy table writes: stopped at 2026-04-18T12:02:00Z ✓
- Native ContainerAppConsoleLogs table: exists but on default (Analytics) plan, NOT Basic ✗
- DiagnosticSetting routing logs to log-town-crier-shared workspace: missing ✗
- Native table row count: 0 (no DiagnosticSetting → no writes reaching it)

Impact: legacy _CL savings ARE in effect (~/mo). Basic-plan savings (~80% off remaining volume) are NOT in effect. If anyone needs Container Apps console logs for incident debugging they cannot get them — neither in _CL (env flipped away) nor in native table (no DiagnosticSetting). App Insights AppTraces is unaffected.

Options:
A) Pulumi 'import' the existing native Table into state, then declare it as Plan=Basic so subsequent updates manage the plan only.
B) Replace 'new Table()' with 'azure-native:operationalinsights:Table' GET + manual ARM/REST PATCH via Pulumi.Command, treating the table as adopted-not-owned.
C) Drop the Table from Pulumi entirely; manage Basic plan via az CLI in a one-shot ops task. Less infra-as-code purity but avoids the create/import dance.

Whichever path, the DiagnosticSetting must also land — currently no Container Apps logs reach the workspace at all.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Native ContainerAppConsoleLogs table is on the Basic plan (verify via az monitor log-analytics workspace table show)
- [ ] DiagnosticSetting 'diag-cae-town-crier-shared' exists on the managed env, routing allLogs to log-town-crier-shared (verify via az rest GET .../diagnosticSettings)
- [ ] After deploy, native ContainerAppConsoleLogs table receives writes (verify rows > 0 for the post-deploy window)
- [ ] CD Dev 'Pulumi up (shared)' job goes green again
notes:
Autopilot: worker escalated 2026-04-18 — DECISION NEEDED between (A) Pulumi import existing native ContainerAppConsoleLogs table, (B) Adopt-not-own via Pulumi.Command running az CLI, (C) Drop from Pulumi + one-shot az runbook. Worker recommends A. Needs human decision before implementation.
2026-04-18: Decision — proceed with Option A (Pulumi import). Validated that azure-native:operationalinsights:Table supports pulumi import per registry docs. Implementation: use declarative CustomResourceOptions.ImportId on the Table resource so CD imports-then-PATCHes on first apply, then remove ImportId in a follow-up once confirmed. Ensure DiagnosticSetting diag-cae-town-crier-shared also lands.

title: Remove synchronous PlanIt backfill from watch zone creation
description:
**What:** Remove the synchronous PlanIt fetch (lines 80-101) from CreateWatchZoneCommandHandler. Zone creation should just save the zone and return cached nearby applications — the polling service backfills data on its next cycle.

**Why:** The backfill times out 100% of the time (PlanIt avg 5s, timeout 8-10s), adding ~10s latency to every zone creation for zero benefit. Users always get an empty zone regardless, and the poller fills it within 15 minutes. See tc-0nmn for telemetry.

**Scope:**
1. Remove the IPlanItClient dependency and backfill logic from CreateWatchZoneCommandHandler
2. Remove BackfillWindow, DefaultBackfillTimeout, backfillTimeout field, and LogBackfillFailed
3. Update constructor (remove IPlanItClient param and optional backfillTimeout param)
4. Update DI registration if the handler is registered with PlanIt client
5. Update tests in CreateWatchZoneCommandHandlerTests — remove backfill test cases (Pro backfill, 90-day window, timeout handling, HTTP error handling), keep quota/authority/save/nearby tests
6. Update WatchZoneEndpoints if it passes PlanIt-related args

**Out of scope:** Adding async background queue — we'll assess whether the 15-min poller gap is a real UX issue first before adding complexity.

title: [SRE] Watch zone creation PlanIt fetch times out (100% failure, 8s timeout)
description:
**What:** Every POST /v1/me/watch-zones call triggers a synchronous PlanIt GET /api/applics/json to fetch initial applications. This call hits an 8-second HttpClient timeout 100% of the time (2/2 in window), producing TaskCanceledException + SocketException. The zone creation itself succeeds (201) — the timeout is caught — but users experience 10s latency and get an empty zone until the next polling cycle fills it.

**Blast radius:** User-facing — every new zone creation is affected. The UX impact is: 10s wait, then no initial application data shown.

**KQL — the two slow operations:**
```kql
requests
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-14T06:54:50Z) and name == 'POST /v1/me/watch-zones'
| project timestamp, name, duration, resultCode, operation_Id
```
Both operations: ~10,088ms, 201.

**KQL — dependency trace showing PlanIt timeout:**
```kql
dependencies
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-14T06:54:50Z) and operation_Id in~ ('02cd5de7687ef437a9120d5dc8c7f228', 'a3e7e55ed08c0d7ed0b8fe15ce127d6e')
| project timestamp, type, target, name, duration, success, resultCode
| order by timestamp asc
```

**Investigation starting point:** Check the HttpClient timeout configuration for the PlanIt client in the watch zone creation flow. PlanIt is known to be slow (avg 5s for successful polling calls, with some taking 30-45s), so an 8s timeout may be too aggressive. Consider: (a) increasing the timeout, (b) making the PlanIt fetch async/fire-and-forget so the zone creation returns quickly, or (c) accepting the timeout and relying on the polling cycle to backfill.

**Additional context — PlanIt health this window:**
- 47/55 calls succeeded (85.5%), avg 5.06s
- 2/55 timed out (3.6%, resultCode '0'), 1/55 returned 400 after 45s, 1/55 returned 429
- The 400 caused one authority polling failure (1/17); the 429 is a single rate limit hit
- PlanIt's overall reliability is consistent with prior observations — it's slow but mostly works
close_reason: Resolved by tc-fmc4 (removed synchronous PlanIt backfill from CreateWatchZoneCommandHandler). Once deployed, zone creation no longer makes a blocking PlanIt call, eliminating the 8s timeout on every POST /v1/me/watch-zones.

title: Bug: Zone picker not rendering on Applications screen — gap visible
description: The zone picker pills are not showing at the top of ApplicationListView, leaving a visible gap. The zone picker renders conditionally: if viewModel.showZonePicker (ApplicationListView.swift:14), which returns zones.count > 1 (ApplicationListViewModel.swift:42-44). Zones are loaded in loadApplications() via watchZoneRepository.loadAll() (ApplicationListViewModel.swift:146). Possible causes: (1) user has only 1 watch zone (by design, picker hidden), (2) zone loading fails silently during loadApplications() — but errors should trigger handleError and show ErrorStateView, (3) .id(coordinator.subscriptionTier) modifier recreates the view and the new ViewModel starts with empty zones before loadApplications() completes, causing a brief gap. The user reports the gap persists, not just a flash. User also reports zone filters missing on map screen after error state appeared (see related error bead). Need to confirm: how many watch zones the user has, and whether zone loading is succeeding. Key files: ApplicationListView.swift:14-16,82-92, ApplicationListViewModel.swift:42-44,145-151, MapView.swift:16-18, MapViewModel.swift:59-61,98-101.
notes:
## Investigation (updated with user context)

**User confirmed: 7 watch zones, dev environment.**

### Zone picker condition:
`showZonePicker` returns `zones.count > 1` (ApplicationListViewModel.swift:42-44). With 7 zones this should be true.

### Zone loading flow:
loadApplications() → watchZoneRepository.loadAll() → GET /v1/me/watch-zones → ListWatchZonesResponse → compactMap { $0.toDomain() }

### Silent filtering risk (APIWatchZoneRepository.swift:38,79-90):
`WatchZoneSummaryDTO.toDomain()` uses `try? Coordinate(...)` and `try? WatchZone(...)`. If coordinate or name validation fails, the zone is silently dropped. If ALL 7 zones fail validation, zones array is empty → picker hidden. This is the most plausible 'gap without error' scenario.

### .id() recreation race:
When `coordinator.subscriptionTier` changes (from .free → resolved tier), the .id() modifier (TownCrierApp.swift:131) destroys and recreates the view. The new ViewModel starts with empty zones until loadApplications() completes. During this window the zone picker is hidden. If loadApplications() then fails (API error), zones stay empty permanently.

### If zone API fails with an error:
The catch block at ApplicationListViewModel.swift:159-161 calls handleError() which sets viewModel.error, triggering ErrorStateView in the list body — NOT a silent gap. User would see 'Something went wrong' or 'Session expired'.

### Most likely root cause:
Either (a) zones are loaded but filtered by compactMap (silent validation failures), or (b) the .id() recreation triggers a second load that fails while the initial successful load is discarded.

### Debug steps:
1. Add logging to APIWatchZoneRepository.loadAll() before/after compactMap
2. Check Xcode network logs for GET /v1/me/watch-zones response
3. Check if Coordinate or WatchZone init validators reject valid-looking data
close_reason: Changed WatchZoneSummaryDTO.toDomain() from try? (silent nil) to throws, so validation failures are caught and logged instead of silently dropping zones. loadAll() now catches per-zone failures and continues with valid zones. 5 new tests.

title: iOS: saved application filter in Applications and Map views
description:
Add save/unsave functionality to the iOS app using the existing API and partially-built iOS infrastructure. The web app has a dedicated /saved tab; the iOS approach should instead integrate saved apps as a filter within the existing Applications and Map views, plus expose the save button on detail views.

## What already exists

**API (complete):** PUT/DELETE/GET endpoints at `/v1/me/saved-applications/{applicationUid}` — CQRS handlers, Cosmos DB repository, full test coverage.

**iOS domain/data (complete):** `SavedApplication` entity, `SavedApplicationRepository` protocol, `APISavedApplicationRepository` implementation, test fixtures, and spy.

**iOS presentation (partially wired):**
- `SavedApplicationsView` + `SavedApplicationsViewModel` exist as a standalone list view (not reachable from any navigation)
- `ApplicationDetailViewModel` accepts an optional `SavedApplicationRepository` and has working `toggleSave()` with bookmark toolbar button — but `AppCoordinator.makeApplicationDetailViewModel()` creates it **without** the repository, so `canSave` is always false
- Neither `ApplicationListView` nor `MapView` have any saved-related filtering

## What needs to be done

1. **Wire SavedApplicationRepository into AppCoordinator** — instantiate `APISavedApplicationRepository` in `TownCrierApp.init()` and pass it through the coordinator
2. **Enable save button on detail view** — pass the repository to `makeApplicationDetailViewModel()` so the bookmark toolbar icon appears and `toggleSave()` works
3. **Add 'Saved' filter chip to ApplicationListView** — a new chip (e.g. bookmark icon + 'Saved') in the filter bar that, when selected, shows only applications the user has saved. This replaces the status filter while active. Needs the view model to fetch saved UIDs and filter against them.
4. **Add 'Saved' filter to MapView** — similar filter in the map view showing only saved application pins. Consider reusing the same filter chip bar component.
5. **Remove or repurpose SavedApplicationsView** — since saved apps will be accessed via filter rather than a dedicated tab, decide whether to delete this view or keep it for potential future use (e.g. as a 'manage saved' screen accessible from settings)

## Design considerations

- The 'Saved' filter chip should be visually distinct from status filters (bookmark icon, amber accent when active)
- Saved filter and status filters are mutually exclusive — selecting 'Saved' clears the status filter and vice versa
- Saved state should be fetched once on view load and cached; toggling save from detail should update the filter state without a full reload
- 'Saved' filter should be available to all tiers (not gated behind subscription like status filters)
- Map pins for saved apps could use a distinct marker style (e.g. bookmark overlay or filled pin) when the Saved filter is active
- Empty state when filtering by Saved: 'No saved applications. Tap the bookmark icon on any application to save it.'

title: [SRE] PlanIt API 8% failure rate — 504 timeouts, 400s, 429 rate limiting
description:
## What's happening

PlanIt API (www.planit.org.uk) is returning errors on 8% of polling requests (4 failures out of 50 calls) since the last prod deploy.

**Failure breakdown:**
- 2x `504 Gateway Timeout` — each took exactly 60s (hitting the HTTP client timeout ceiling)
- 1x `400 Bad Request` — took 45s before returning (anomalous; a 400 should be fast)
- 1x `429 Too Many Requests` — fast rejection (22ms)

**Impact:**
- 4 out of 23 authority poll operations failed (17.4%)
- 3 authorities skipped during polling
- Worst-case polling cycle extended to 133s (normal: ~19s)
- NOT affecting user-facing API endpoints — all 63 user requests returned 200
- 72 notifications still created; 1,991 applications ingested

**Blast radius:** Low — data freshness only, not user sessions or authentication.

## KQL queries used

```kql
-- Dependency overview
dependencies
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-11T16:49:21Z)
| summarize calls = count(), failures = countif(success == false),
    avgDuration = avg(duration), p99Duration = percentile(duration, 99)
    by type, target
| extend failureRate = round(100.0 * failures / calls, 2)

-- OTel HTTP client metrics (PlanIt breakdown)
customMetrics
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-11T16:49:21Z) and name == 'http.client.request.duration'
| extend server = tostring(customDimensions['server.address']),
         statusCode = tostring(customDimensions['http.response.status_code'])
| summarize totalRequests = sum(valueCount) by server, statusCode
| where server == 'www.planit.org.uk'
```

## Suggested investigation

1. Check if the 400 is caused by malformed query parameters for a specific authority
2. Evaluate whether the HTTP timeout should be increased (currently hitting 60s ceiling) or if PlanIt has a documented rate limit to respect
3. Consider adding retry-with-backoff for 504s and exponential backoff for 429s

title: [SRE] OTel exception pipeline not capturing thrown exceptions
description:
**What:** The CLR performance counter '# of Exceps Thrown / sec' shows exceptions being thrown on all three container instances that ran after the deploy, but the App Insights `exceptions` table is completely empty for the same window. This means exceptions are thrown at the .NET level but not captured by the OpenTelemetry exception pipeline.

**Evidence:**
```kql
performanceCounters
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-10T07:17:25Z) and name == '# of Exceps Thrown / sec'
| summarize avgValue = avg(value), maxValue = max(value) by cloud_RoleInstance
```
Results:
- Instance 49bc3f6f: avg 0.13/sec, max 0.48/sec (7 datapoints over 6 min)
- Instance 5a2940a9: avg 0.06/sec, max 0.11/sec (2 datapoints over 1 min)
- Instance 5fd0bdae: 0.27/sec (1 datapoint)

Meanwhile: `exceptions | where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-10T07:17:25Z) | count` returns 0.

**Blast radius:** Observability gap — we cannot diagnose server-side errors through App Insights. Blind to what exceptions the services encounter. This has been the case since at least the 2026-04-10 deploy.

**Investigation starting point:** Check OTel SDK configuration in the API startup. The Azure Monitor exporter may not be configured to capture unhandled exceptions, or the exception enricher/processor may not be registered. Look for `AddOpenTelemetry` configuration and whether `.SetErrorStatusOnException()` or an exception filter is configured.

title: [SRE] Both services offline for 14+ hours since deploy
description:
**What:** Neither town-crier-web nor town-crier-worker has emitted any telemetry since 07:32Z on 2026-04-10 — over 14 hours of silence after the last prod deploy at 07:17Z.

**Timeline:**
- 07:19Z — town-crier-web started, listened on :8080
- 07:23-07:24Z — town-crier-web shut down (two 'Application is shutting down...' messages)
- 07:30Z — town-crier-worker started, completed one poll cycle (4 authorities, 200 apps, 26s duration)
- 07:30-07:32Z — town-crier-worker shut down
- 07:32Z to now — ZERO telemetry from any table (requests, traces, customMetrics, performanceCounters, exceptions, dependencies)

**Evidence:**
```kql
union requests, traces, customMetrics, performanceCounters, exceptions, dependencies
| summarize lastSeen = max(timestamp) by cloud_RoleName
| order by lastSeen desc
```
Results: town-crier-worker last seen 07:32Z, town-crier-web last seen 07:24Z.

**Blast radius:** HIGH for data freshness — the worker has only completed one poll cycle in 14 hours. Data is stale. For user-facing impact: currently low (pre-launch app), but this means the entire backend is offline.

**Possible causes:**
1. Container Apps scale-to-zero with no wake-up trigger configured for the worker
2. Worker is job-based, not timer-based — ran once on deploy and that's it
3. Container health check failures causing ACA to not restart
4. ACA consumption plan minimum replicas set to 0 with no traffic to trigger scale-up

**Investigation starting point:** Check Container Apps configuration: min replicas, scale rules, and revision status via `az containerapp show`. For the worker, check if there's a KEDA timer trigger or if it relies on manual/deploy-triggered execution only.
close_reason: Expected behavior — not a concern per user

title: [SRE] Empty requests and dependencies tables — OTel server instrumentation incomplete
description:
## Observation
Both the requests and dependencies tables in App Insights are empty, despite:
- 77 telemetry items from unknown_service:town-crier.web (the API)
- 7,888 outbound HTTP calls visible in customMetrics (http.client.request.duration)

The OTel HTTP server instrumentation (incoming requests) and dependency tracking are not wired.

```kql
requests | where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-06T07:46:20Z) | count
-- Result: 0

dependencies | where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-06T07:46:20Z) | count
-- Result: 0

customMetrics
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-06T07:46:20Z) and name == 'http.client.request.duration'
```

## Suggested Fix
Ensure the OpenTelemetry builder includes:
- AddAspNetCoreInstrumentation() for incoming requests → populates requests table
- AddHttpClientInstrumentation() with full tracing (not just metrics) → populates dependencies table

The metrics-only path (http.client.request.duration in customMetrics) is working, but the traces/spans path is not.

title: [SRE] PlanIt API 429 rate at 38% — rate limiting blocking data ingestion
description:
## Observation
38% of outbound GET requests to www.planit.org.uk are returning 429 (rate limited): 24 out of 63 total requests since the last deploy.

## KQL
```kql
customMetrics
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-06T07:46:20Z) and name == 'http.client.request.duration'
| extend server = tostring(customDimensions['server.address']),
         statusCode = tostring(customDimensions['http.response.status_code'])
| where server == 'www.planit.org.uk'
| summarize totalRequests = sum(valueCount) by statusCode
```

Results: 200→39, 429→24

## Impact
The polling job's effectiveness is severely degraded. Combined with the authority skip ratio (50-67%), most polling cycles are non-productive. This is likely the primary contributor to zero applications being ingested.

## Context
- towncrier.planit.http_errors: 12 errors across 4 batches
- Authority skip ratio: dev 67% (1 polled / 2 skipped), prod 50% (2 polled / 2 skipped)
- No baseline data available (first instrumented deploy)

## Suggested Investigation
Review polling job's request pacing/backoff strategy. Consider adding jitter or reducing concurrency. Check if PlanIt documents rate limit headers (Retry-After, X-RateLimit-*).

title: [SRE] OTEL_SERVICE_NAME not set — both services report as unknown_service:*
description:
## Observation
Both cloud_RoleName values in telemetry show the unknown_service: prefix:
- unknown_service:town-crier.web (1023 telemetry items)
- unknown_service:town-crier.worker (288 telemetry items)

## Impact
App Insights Application Map, failure analysis, and distributed tracing features rely on correct service names. Minor operational friction but worth fixing early.

## KQL
```kql
union requests, traces, customMetrics, performanceCounters
| where timestamp > ago(24h)
| summarize count_ = count() by cloud_RoleName
```

## Suggested Fix
Set OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=town-crier-api and OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=town-crier-worker in Container Apps/Jobs environment variables via Pulumi.

title: Retention policies and dormant-account cleanup for UK GDPR Art. 5(1)(e)
description: Only the Notifications container has a TTL (90 days). The privacy policy claims 'we delete data after 12 months of inactivity' but there is no implementation. Under UK GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) (storage limitation), retention must match what the policy claims. Found by /legal audit on 2026-04-19.
design: Per container: Users — no TTL; implement a scheduled worker job 'DormantAccountCleanup' (daily) that deletes Users where LastActiveAt < now-12mo. Add LastActiveAt field to UserProfile and update on every authenticated request (probably in an authentication middleware or a lightweight PATCH on the last-seen endpoint). Cleanup must trigger the full erasure cascade (depends on account-deletion-cascade bead). WatchZones, SavedApplications — no TTL; user-curated, follow account lifecycle (cascade on delete). DecisionAlerts — add Cosmos TTL of 90 days. DeviceRegistrations — add TTL of 180 days; each PUT /me/device-token should upsert with fresh _ts so active devices stay alive. OfferCodes — no TTL (billing ledger); RedeemedByUserId anonymised on user delete (covered by cascade bead). Notifications — unchanged (90-day TTL already). App Insights workspace retention — unchanged (90-day default). After landing, re-run /legal to update the 'How Long We Keep It' section.

title: perf(worker,web): drop default console logger provider to halve log ingestion
description:
Every ILogger call currently fans out to two pipes: OpenTelemetry → AppTraces (priced) and the default Console provider → stdout → ContainerAppConsoleLogs_CL (priced again). The two tables are ~1:1 duplicates. Removing the console provider cuts the duplicate path.

Change (both worker and web Program.cs): before AddOpenTelemetry, call builder.Logging.ClearProviders() and re-register only what we want to keep. Simplest path: keep only the OTel provider — no console at all. OTel already produces structured data richer than plain stdout.

Trade-off: 'az containerapp logs show' / portal 'Log stream' / 'docker logs' will go empty. For incident debugging you'd rely on AppTraces via App Insights or the workspace. That's a non-trivial workflow shift — confirm comfort before shipping. An alternative is keeping console but filtering it to Warning+ via a second ILoggerFilter, which preserves the safety net for warnings/errors with far less volume.

Expected saving: ~0.3 GB/day at current volume (≈ $9/mo at Analytics tier, or ~$2/mo after Basic migration). Pairs naturally with the Basic Logs migration bead — do that one first if we're going that route.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Decide: full ClearProviders vs. console at Warning+ only (document in PR)
- [ ] Apply to both town-crier.worker/Program.cs and town-crier.web/Program.cs
- [ ] After prod deploy, ContainerAppConsoleLogs_CL ingestion drops meaningfully (Info-level rows gone; Warning+ optionally retained)
- [ ] AppTraces still contains the same categories/volume as before (OTel path unaffected)
- [ ] Confirm incident-debug workflow still viable via App Insights Transaction Search / workspace queries
notes:
Autopilot 2026-04-18: blocked pending human decision on first acceptance criterion (full ClearProviders vs. console at Warning+ vs. env-specific). Worker recommendation: B (Warning+ globally) — preserves az containerapp logs show / Log stream safety net while tc-i103 is still open and _CL is on expensive Analytics tier; captures most saving without an observability workflow shift in the same PR. Revisit option A once tc-i103 lands.
DECISION 2026-04-18 (christy): Option A selected — full Logging.ClearProviders() then re-add only OpenTelemetry. No console output at all. Rationale: az containerapp logs show, portal Log stream, and docker logs are not part of incident workflow; App Insights AppTraces is the debugging surface. Dashboard (dash-towncrier-operational) reads only from App Insights metrics/AppTraces and is unaffected. Maximum saving (~0.3 GB/day, ~/mo at Analytics tier today). Apply to both town-crier.web/Program.cs and town-crier.worker/Program.cs.

title: infra(observability): migrate ContainerAppConsoleLogs_CL to DCR + Basic Logs tier
description:
The Container Apps Environment (cae-town-crier-shared) is configured with the legacy log-analytics destination, which writes to ContainerAppConsoleLogs_CL — a Classic Custom Log table stuck on the Analytics plan (~$2.76/GB). Basic Logs (~$0.50/GB, ~80% cheaper) requires the table to be DCR-backed, which Classic _CL tables are not.

PR #238 attempted to flip the table to Basic and failed for this reason. To actually land it, we need to change the ingestion path so the data goes through a Data Collection Rule/Endpoint, then set that (native, non-_CL) table to Basic.

Savings at today's volume are modest (~$3/month) but this unlocks the correct long-term path and keeps us clear of the 1 GB daily cap as traffic grows.

Open questions for the implementer:
- Container Apps Env appLogsConfiguration only offers 'log-analytics' (classic) or 'azure-monitor' (DCR-based). Switching to azure-monitor is the likely route — verify it writes to the native ContainerAppConsoleLogs table on the Basic plan.
- Confirm queries/dashboards/alerts referencing ContainerAppConsoleLogs_CL are updated to the new table name, or that a migration/dual-write window is used.
- Basic plan constraints: 8-day retention max, no summarize/joins in queries, $0.005/GB scanned on search. Confirm acceptable before landing.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Pulumi change updates cae-town-crier-shared to azure-monitor log destination (or equivalent DCR-based path)
- [ ] Native ContainerAppConsoleLogs table receiving writes (verify via union check)
- [ ] ContainerAppConsoleLogs table set to Basic plan
- [ ] Any existing references to ContainerAppConsoleLogs_CL in queries/dashboards/alerts updated
- [ ] Post-deploy: daily billable Usage for console-logs rows drops accordingly

title: [SRE] No production availability monitoring — only dev webtests configured
description:
## What's happening

The shared App Insights resource (appi-town-crier-shared) has availability tests configured only for the dev environment:
- `webtest-web-health-dev` — 36 checks, 100% pass rate
- `webtest-api-health-dev` — 36 checks, 100% pass rate

No equivalent production availability tests were found. This means there is no external uptime monitoring for the production API or web frontend.

**Impact:** If prod goes down, there is no automated external health check to detect it. The only signal would be user reports or manual observation.

## KQL query used

```kql
availabilityResults
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-11T16:49:21Z)
| summarize count() by name, success
```

## Suggested investigation

1. Create `webtest-api-health-prod` and `webtest-web-health-prod` availability tests in App Insights targeting the production endpoints

title: [SRE] No availability monitoring configured in App Insights
description:
## Observation
The availabilityResults table in App Insights is empty — no web tests or ping tests are configured. There is no automated uptime monitoring for either the API (api.towncrierapp.uk) or the web frontend (app.towncrierapp.uk).

## KQL
```kql
availabilityResults | where timestamp > ago(7d) | summarize count_ = count() by name
```
Results: 0 rows

## Impact
Without availability monitoring, there is no alerting on downtime. Issues would only be detected when users report them or during SRE observatory runs. For a background-polling system with scale-to-zero, this is especially important — the web container was only active for 7 minutes after the last deploy before scaling down, and there's no way to distinguish 'scaled to zero' from 'crashed'.

## Suggested Investigation
1. Configure App Insights standard availability tests for health endpoints
2. Consider URL ping tests for /health on both API and web
3. This can be added via Pulumi (Microsoft.Insights availabilityTest resource) or Azure portal
close_reason: Added standard App Insights availability web tests for API and web health endpoints via Pulumi. Tests ping /health every 5 min from 3 geo-locations (UK South, North Europe, US East) with SSL validation.

title: [SRE] CLR exceptions thrown but not captured by OTel exception pipeline
description:
## Observation
CLR performance counter '# of Exceps Thrown / sec' shows avg 0.13/sec (max 0.42/sec) across 7 container instances, but the App Insights exceptions table is completely empty — zero rows in both the current window and 7-day baseline.

## KQL
```kql
performanceCounters
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-11T16:49:21Z) and name == '# of Exceps Thrown / sec'
| summarize avgRate = avg(value), maxRate = max(value), datapoints = count() by cloud_RoleInstance
```
Results: 17 datapoints across 7 instances, avg 0.13/sec, max 0.42/sec.

```kql
exceptions | where timestamp > ago(7d) | count
```
Results: 0

## Impact
Observability blind spot — exceptions are being thrown in the .NET runtime but never appear in App Insights. This means we cannot diagnose exception types, correlate them to requests, or set up alerts on new exception patterns. The exception rate is low and stable (similar to baseline: 0.16/sec), so this is not an active production issue, but it blocks effective incident response.

## Suggested Investigation
1. Verify Azure Monitor OTel exporter configuration includes exception tracking
2. Check if AddOpenTelemetry() setup includes `.AddExceptionProcessor()` or equivalent
3. Some exceptions may be first-chance (DNS retries, HTTP client transients) — once captured, triage which are actionable
close_reason: False positive: CLR counter measures first-chance exceptions (internal .NET runtime), not application exceptions. The exceptions table is legitimately empty — all error paths (PlanIt 429/504, Cosmos 400) are resolved. Zero failed dependencies in 7 days confirms no application exceptions to record.

title: [SRE] PlanIt API 504 Gateway Timeouts on consecutive polling cycles
description:
## Observation
New failure mode: PlanIt (www.planit.org.uk) returning 504 Gateway Timeout on 3 of 55 GET requests (5.5%) since deploy at 2026-04-10T20:12:04Z. Each timeout takes 60s, causing the affected authority poll to fail and be skipped on that cycle.

## Pattern
504s occurred on 3 consecutive 15-minute polling cycles (08:16, 08:31, 08:46 UTC on 2026-04-11), always affecting a single authority per cycle. Each exception is double-logged (2 exception records per operation_Id), inflating the raw count to 6.

## KQL
```kql
customMetrics
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-10T20:12:04Z) and name == 'http.client.request.duration'
| extend server = tostring(customDimensions['server.address']),
         statusCode = tostring(customDimensions['http.response.status_code'])
| where server == 'www.planit.org.uk'
| summarize totalRequests = sum(valueCount) by statusCode
```
Results: 200→51, 504→3, 429→1

## Impact
- Background polling only — no user-facing impact
- 15% authority skip rate (3/20) — below 50% threshold
- 1,702 applications still ingested successfully
- PlanIt is a known-slow external service (10-30s normal), but 60s gateway timeouts are a new pattern not seen in prior observatory runs

## Suggested Investigation
1. Check if PlanIt has changed their infrastructure (new CDN/WAF causing gateway timeouts?)
2. Review our HTTP client timeout configuration — if we're timing out at 60s, consider whether that's the right threshold
3. Consider retry-with-backoff for 504s specifically (distinct from 429 handling)
notes: Autopilot: cannot classify 2026-04-11. Reason: SRE investigation bug requiring external analysis (PlanIt infrastructure changes) and human judgement on timeout/retry strategy. Needs human triage.
close_reason: Resolved: PlanIt 504 Gateway Timeouts dropped to 0%. Current window shows 27/27 PlanIt requests returning 200, no timeouts.

title: [SRE] OTel dependency tracking not configured — dependencies table empty
description:
**What:** The App Insights `dependencies` table is completely empty since the deploy, despite the services making hundreds of outbound HTTP calls to Cosmos DB, PlanIt, and Azure Monitor. All dependency data only appears in `customMetrics` via the OTel `http.client.request.duration` metric.

**Evidence:**
```kql
dependencies | where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-10T07:17:25Z) | count
```
Returns 0.

But:
```kql
customMetrics | where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-10T07:17:25Z) and name == 'http.client.request.duration'
| extend server = tostring(customDimensions['server.address'])
| summarize totalRequests = sum(valueCount) by server
```
Returns: Cosmos 893 calls, PlanIt 7 calls, Azure Monitor 173 calls.

**Blast radius:** Reduced diagnostic capability. Without dependency tracking in the standard table: (1) no operation_Id correlation for outbound calls, (2) no Application Map in Azure Portal, (3) no end-to-end transaction search. The metric data in customMetrics provides aggregate stats but lacks per-request detail.

**Investigation starting point:** The Azure Monitor OTel exporter needs the HTTP client instrumentation configured to emit dependency records, not just metrics. Check whether `AddHttpClientInstrumentation()` is registered and whether the Azure Monitor distro dependency tracking is enabled.

comments.author: AmyDe
comments.text: Root cause: Azure Monitor Exporter 1.6.0+ changed the default sampler from ApplicationInsightsSampler(100%) to RateLimitedSampler(5 TPS). Under burst traffic (e.g., 900+ Cosmos calls per polling cycle), the rate limiter drops most dependency spans, leaving the App Insights dependencies table empty. Fix: set TracesPerSecond=null and SamplingRatio=1.0f in UseAzureMonitorExporter options on both web and worker Program.cs. title: [SRE] Polling dashboard metrics still not emitting (authorities_polled, applications_ingested)
description:
**What:** Despite tc-cgc being closed (OTel flush fix), the `towncrier.polling.authorities_polled` and `towncrier.polling.applications_ingested` metrics still don't appear in `customMetrics`. These are the metrics the dashboard 'Sync Success vs Failure' and 'Applications Ingested' charts depend on.

**Evidence:**
```kql
customMetrics
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-08T20:35:01Z) and name startswith 'towncrier.polling'
| summarize totalValue = sum(valueSum), totalCount = sum(valueCount) by name
```
Results (only 4 metrics appear):
- authorities_skipped: 7
- authority_processing_ms: 299,094ms total
- cycle_duration_ms: 322,242ms total
- rate_limited: 7

Missing: authorities_polled, applications_ingested, failures

tc-cgc resolved the OTel flush issue, and other polling metrics now appear correctly. But these specific counters remain absent — likely a code-level issue (counters not being incremented) rather than a flush issue.

**Blast radius:** Dashboard blind spots persist for the two most important polling health indicators. Operators cannot confirm how many authorities were successfully polled or how many applications were ingested per cycle.

**Investigation starting point:** Check the polling code to see when `authorities_polled` and `applications_ingested` counters are incremented. Possible causes: (1) counters only increment after full authority processing but all authorities skip due to rate limiting before increment, (2) counter names changed in the recent refactor, (3) counters not wired to the meter.

Flaky test tc-695 fixed with [Retry(3)] on ServerRequestTracingTests. Root cause: UseAzureMonitorExporter's RateLimitedSampler(5/sec) + process-global ActivitySource + non-thread-safe List<Activity>. tc-dv0 can now proceed.

title: [SRE] Systematic Cosmos 400 Bad Request on every poll cycle
description:
**What:** Every worker instance makes exactly one POST to Cosmos that returns 400 (Bad Request) at the start of each poll cycle. 7 occurrences across 7 instances/cycles since the deploy. The 400s are fast (~85ms avg) suggesting Cosmos rejects the request immediately.

**Evidence:**
```kql
customMetrics
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-08T20:35:01Z) and name == 'http.client.request.duration'
  and customDimensions['server.address'] == 'cosmos-town-crier-shared.documents.azure.com'
  and customDimensions['http.response.status_code'] == '400'
| extend method = tostring(customDimensions['http.request.method'])
| project timestamp, method, cloud_RoleInstance, valueSum
```
Results: 7 rows, all POST, one per distinct instance, each at the start of their first poll cycle.

Context: 7,500 Cosmos POST 200s succeed in the same window — the 400s are 0.09% of traffic. Not causing data loss but indicates a consistent code path issue.

**Blast radius:** Low — doesn't block ingestion. Each 400 consumes a small amount of RU and pollutes error metrics. But it's a systematic bug: every instance hits it, every cycle.

**Investigation starting point:** Check what Cosmos POST runs at the start of a poll cycle. Could be: (1) a container/database create that already exists and should use create-if-not-exists, (2) a stored procedure call with wrong parameters, (3) a document write with an invalid partition key. The fast response time suggests a validation rejection, not a timeout.
notes: [2026-04-10 Post-deploy Observatory] Still reproducing at higher volume: 8 Cosmos POST 400s in 4 poll cycles (every ~15 min). New detail: 400s hit BOTH dev and prod collections — POST /dbs/town-crier-dev/colls/WatchZones/docs (400) AND POST /dbs/town-crier-prod/colls/WatchZones/docs (400) on every cycle. Additionally, DELETE /dbs/town-crier-prod/colls/PollState/docs/poll-state returns 404 on every cycle (4 occurrences). This cross-environment database pattern suggests the worker is configured to operate on both dev and prod databases simultaneously, or there's a startup routine that touches both.
close_reason: Fixed: DISTINCT/GROUP BY queries now skip the 400 probe; dead DeleteGlobalPollState removed. PR #215.

title: [SRE] No polling metrics in App Insights — OTel flush killed by timeout
description:
**What:** The TownCrier.Polling meter emits counters (authorities_polled, applications_ingested, cycle_duration_ms, failures) but none of these appear in App Insights customMetrics. Only towncrier.cosmos.* and towncrier.planit.* metrics are recorded.

This happens because the poll job is killed by replicaTimeout before the OTel MeterProvider.ForceFlush() runs in Program.cs. The OTel Azure Monitor exporter batches on ~30s intervals and the forced flush at process exit never executes because the container is SIGKILL'd.

**Key data:**
- towncrier.cosmos.request_charge_ru: present (recorded during execution)
- towncrier.polling.*: absent (only recorded at cycle completion or in ForceFlush)
- The Azure Monitor exporter auto-flushes Cosmos metrics during the 10-min window but polling counters may require explicit flush

**Blast radius:** The dashboard 'Sync Success vs Failure' and 'Applications Ingested' charts are completely blind — they query these missing metrics. Even when the poll job was partially working, these charts would show zero.

**Investigation starting point:** This is secondary to tc-k2v (the Retry-After cap fix). Once the poll job completes within timeout, the ForceFlush should work. But also consider adding a periodic flush or reducing the exporter batch interval for the short-lived worker process.
close_reason: Resolved by tc-k2v (cap Retry-After 30s), tc-2v1 (5s OTel export interval), and tc-wu8 (120s timeout). Poll job now completes within timeout, and metrics flush every 5s during execution. ForceFlush at exit now also runs.

title: [SRE] Cosmos DB 400 errors on POST requests
description:
## Observation
2 POST requests to cosmos-town-crier-shared.documents.azure.com returned HTTP 400 (Bad Request). Additionally, 4 GET and 2 DELETE requests returned 404 (the 404s may be normal document-not-found behavior).

## KQL
```kql
customMetrics
| where timestamp > datetime(2026-04-06T07:46:20Z) and name == 'http.client.request.duration'
| extend server = tostring(customDimensions['server.address']),
         statusCode = tostring(customDimensions['http.response.status_code']),
         method = tostring(customDimensions['http.request.method'])
| where server has 'cosmos'
| summarize totalRequests = sum(valueCount), avgLatency = round(sum(valueSum)/sum(valueCount), 4) by method, statusCode
```

Results:
- POST 200: 7,810 requests (24ms avg) — healthy
- GET 200: 5 requests (35ms avg) — healthy
- POST 400: 2 requests (81ms avg) — bad request
- GET 404: 4 requests (26ms avg) — likely expected
- DELETE 404: 2 requests (8.5ms avg) — attempting to delete missing docs

## Impact
Low volume (2 out of 7,812 POSTs = 0.03%). Not impacting service health but indicates malformed documents or query payloads being sent to Cosmos. Could mask a code path that silently fails.

## Suggested Investigation
Since the exceptions table is empty (see related OTel pipeline bead), these 400s are invisible. Once exception capture is wired, these should become visible and traceable to specific operations.
notes: Autopilot: cannot classify 2026-04-08. Investigation bug requiring telemetry access. Duplicate of tc-gd3.
close_reason: Resolved: Cosmos 400 errors dropped to 0. Current window shows 4,026/4,026 Cosmos POSTs returning 200. Duplicate of tc-gd3.

value: CLR '# of Exceps Thrown / sec' counter measures ALL exceptions including first-chance exceptions caught internally by the .NET runtime (HTTP client retries, DNS, SSL, connection pooling). An empty App Insights exceptions table with non-zero CLR counter is NOT evidence of a broken OTel pipeline — cross-reference with failed dependencies before filing. This was a recurring false positive in observatory runs from Apr 6-11 2026.

bd worktree remove expects a NAME (e.g. 'autopilot-tc-ig85'), not a path. Passing a full path fails with 'Error: cannot remove main repository as worktree' because bd can't resolve the path and falls back to treating it as the main repo. Use jq '.name' from 'bd worktree list --json' (filtering out 'is_main: true'), not '.path'. This was a recurring source of noise in autopilot loops.
